titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Robert Fridenberg Collection of Portrait and Topographical Prints
Fridenberg (Robert) Collection of Portrait and Topographical Prints
titlestmt.author: by Beinecke Staff

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Friday May 22, 2020 at 03:19

did.unittitle: Robert Fridenberg Collection of Portrait and Topographical Prints

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.abstract: A collection of European and American portrait and topographical prints that includes work by printmakers Samuel Arlent Edwards, George Edward Perine, Eugène Sadoux, and others.

langmaterial.language: Captions in English, French, Dutch, and German.

persname.part: Orsay, Alfred Guillaume Gabriel, comte d', 1801-1852

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into five series: I. Portraits, 1500-1910. II. Topographical Views, 1750-1900. III. Book Illustrations. IV. Other Prints. V. Robert Fridenberg Administrative Records.

bioghist.head: Robert Fridenberg Collection of Portrait and Topographical Prints
bioghist.p: The Robert Fridenberg Collection of Portrait and Topographical Prints was purchased in New York by Joseph Verner Reed (1902-1973, Yale 1926) sometime after Fridenberg's death in 1946. Reed, a collector of fine prints, had been a patron of Fridenberg and his successor, Joseph A. Heckel. The prints were moved from New York into an outbuilding at Reed's home in Greenwich, Connecticut, where they remained for more than a decade. In 1957, at his wife's urging because she needed the outbuilding for a potting shed, Reed offered the collection to the Yale University Art Gallery, but finding the contents more historical than fine art, the museum declined. Reed's second offer, to the Yale University Library, was accepted by then-Yale University Librarian James T. Babb. Described in an April 3, 1958, list of "Important Recent Gifts" to the library, the "collection of prints and plates contained in eighty-nine cabinets, two sideboards, eighty-two cartons, and eighty packages" was move into the basement of Sterling Memorial Library. In his 1958 appraisal for Reed, Heckel stated that the collection held "over 500,000 Old Engravings" consisting of historical portraits and views, and English and American drama, that "took over 75 years to accumulate."

The collection remained in the basement until the early 1960s when the Yale library administration decided to bring the history of printing, fine printing, and graphic arts volumes together following models established in 1938 at Harvard University by founding curator Philip Hofer (1898-1984) at Houghton Library's Department of Printing and Graphic Arts, and in 1940 at Princeton University by the printer and typographic designer Elmer Adler (1884-1962) at Firestone Library's Graphic Arts Collection. Dale R. Roylance was hired away from Princeton's Graphic Arts Collection in 1960 as a reference specialist for graphic arts, and in April 1962 a new curatorial position was planned in order to support the library's goal of creating a special collection with a "selective, artistically qualitative approach to printing, illustrating, and book-making." Mentioned as cornerstones of the new Graphic Arts Collection were the collections of fine printing previously donated to Yale by Frank Altschul (1887-1981, Yale 1908) and John Hay Whitney (1904-1982, Yale 1928), and the "Reed prints"; they were to be joined by other materials combed from Sterling Memorial Library's general book stacks. Dale Roylance was made a full curator in 1965, and actively developed, exhibited, and published the collection until he returned to Princeton in 1979; Gay Walker followed him as curator, and served from 1979 until 1991. The collection itself was retitled the Arts of the Book Collection by April 1972 and thereafter was moved into a suite of rooms on the library's ground floor south of the High Street entrance.

At some point after 1987 the Fridenberg prints, then known as the "Vertical File Collection of Plates and Prints," were moved from Sterling's fourth floor to a room just outside Arts of the Book Collection. Partially organized and little used, most of the portrait prints had been rehoused by students in several (generally incomplete) campaigns, and were stored in file cabinets. Oversized prints remained housed inside labeled folders Fridenberg created from extra plates taken from broken folio volumes, primarily sets of the nine-volume Galeries Historiques du Palais de Versailles (Paris: Imprimerie Royale, 1839-1853). The small topographical prints were largely untouched and stayed as they had been in Fridenberg's shop, in geographic-based groupings held within repurposed and relabeled cases of disbound books.

On March 16, 1992, the prints were moved in their file cabinets to Yale's Lewis Walpole Library in Farmington, Connecticut, and placed in an outbuilding. Joan Sussler, curator of prints at the library, mined the collection for images relevant to the Walpole collection. Approximately half of the prints of England and Scotland, and a good selection of English portraits, were extracted and taken into the library's storage area but were not cataloged, processed, or integrated into the Lewis Walpole collection. In 1999 the "Prints and Plates Collection" was acquired by Beinecke Library, packed into cartons, and returned to New Haven; retitled the "General Print Collection," it was given the call number Uncat MS Vault Prints, and a box-level inventory was created without a catalog record in Orbis. In 2012 more refined processing began on the peripatetic collection, and it was once again retitled, this time to acknowledge Robert Fridenberg as its collector. In 2015 the British prints at the Lewis Walpole Library were transferred to Beinecke and added to the collection. Views of London and English towns were either interfiled with those already sorted and filed or added to the end of the existing topographical files. The portraits were left physically together and filed at the end of the topographical files; both were integrated intellectually through listings in the finding aid.

This collection history was compiled through telephone interviews with Joseph Verner Reed Jr. (1937-2016, Yale 1961, son of the donor), former Arts of the Book curators Dale Roylance (1924-2013) and Gay Walker, and former Yale Librarian James R. Tanis (1928-2015, Yale 1951, librarian 1965-1968), in addition to extensive research in the Yale Librarian Records (RU 120) at Manuscripts and Archives, Sterling Memorial Library.
Former call number: Uncat MS Vault Prints

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Robert Fridenberg Collection of Portrait and Topographical Prints. General Collection, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A collection of European and American portrait and topographical prints that includes work by printmakers Samuel Arlent Edwards, George Edward Perine, Eugène Sadoux, and others.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Robert Fridenberg Collection of Portrait and Topographical Prints is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

subject.part: Caricatures and cartoons -- Great Britain -- 19th century
Country homes -- Great Britain -- 18th century -- Pictorial works
Country homes -- Great Britain -- 19th century -- Pictorial works

genreform.part: Steel engravings (visual works) -- United States -- 19th century

persname.part: Bicknell, W. H. W. (William Harry Warren), 1860-1947
Orsay, Alfred Guillaume Gabriel, comte d', 1801-1852

corpname.part: New York (N.Y.). Committee on Centennial Celebration of the Inauguration of Washington, 1789

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Scenes of homes and incidents from life left filed with portraits. Spouses of people with folders are filed with them. In the Royal Figures section, with the exception of Marie Antoinette, consorts are filed with monarchs but not listed.

did.physloc: Also stored in: Oversize, Box X, folder XX

odd.head: General note
odd.p: Includes bound set of trial proofs and prints as issued.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes prospectus slip for the mezzotint and a 1904 brochure for Dawson-Watson's summer painting school, Woodstock, NY

did.unittitle: Hart number 838 (by Charles B. Hall after Hodson)

odd.head: General note
odd.p: Includes a letter from the publisher, Charles H. Lawrence, and sets of numered and signed proofs.

did.unittitle: Portraits by Frederick T. Stuart for J. O. Wright & Co.

odd.head: General note
odd.p: The folder contains numbered artist's proofs signed by Stuart and Wright, an issue of the Rye (N.Y.) Courier containing an article about the print, and a trimmed copy of the commercially issued print.

did.physloc: Also stored in: Oversize, Box XX

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A discrete group of chromolithographed caricatures of notable figures from the late nineteenth and early twentieth century, which were published in Vanity Fair , a weekly magazine with social, literary and political content issued in London between 1868 and 1914. The portraits are filed in alphabetical order by surname.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Filed here are portraits of "any ruler or sovereign bearing the title of king, queen, emperor, empress, or the equivalent of one of these," as defined by the Oxford English Dictionary , who ruled in Europe. England and France are the countries most represented, followed by houses, such as Orange and Nassau. Included here too are members of the Bonaparte family as their titles extended to so many countries. Folders may also contain portraits of rulers' consorts, with the exception of some, such as Marie Antoinette, who received their own folders.
As with other sitters in the collection, proper names were taken from the Library of Congress Name Authority File.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This section contains the Kings and Queens of England. The folders also contain the appropriate spouses, consorts, mistresses, and offspring (e.g., Henry VIII's folders also contain Anne Boleyn and Catherine of Aragon, etc.).

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized in chronological order by period of rule.

did.unittitle: George I, King of Great Britain, 1660-1727
George II, King of Great Britain, 1683-1760
George III, King of Great Britain, 1738-1820
George IV, King of Great Britain, 1762-1830
William IV, King of Great Britain, 1765-1837

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized in chronological order by period of rule.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized in alphabetical order. Queens, consorts, and children are filed with Kings. Prints of queens without kings are filed independently.

Maria Theresa, of Naples-Sicily, Empress, consort of Francis I, Emperor of Austria, -1807

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains images of rulers, military figures, religious leaders, and notable citizens from countries other than Europe, the British Isles, and the United States, though the prints were generally published in those places. Due to their small quantity relative to the rest of the collection, they were isolated for ease of access.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains figures from Greek or Roman history, mainly 1) rulers, military leaders, emperors and senators, and 2) writers, historians and philosophers, including writers whose works we have, though we have little or no concrete historical information of their lives (e.g., Homer). Figures have their own folders if prints were numerous, or the figures were particularly well-known and therefore researchers were likely to look for their names. In the case of writers, folders contain portraits of the writers themselves rather than their subjects (i.e., prints showing images of Homer, not images from the works; for depictions of the Iliad or Odyssey, look under Homer in the Classical Figures Mythological subseries). Folders may also contain images of the figures' wives and children. For information on historical figures, use the Oxford Classical Dictionary Online. Unidentified emperors are filed under "Caesar."

Following the A - Z general files are two folders holding plates from Thomas Worlidge's three-volume work, A Select Collection of Drawings, from curious antique gems, most of them in the possession of the nobility and gentry of this kingdom. Etched after the manner of Rembrandt. (London and Bath, 1768). Many have images of historical and mythological figures, as well as other historical iconography.

did.unittitle: Plates from Thomas Worlidge, A Select Collection of Drawings, from curious antique gems (London, Printed by D. Leach for M. Worlidge and M. Wicksteed, Bath, 1768)
Augustus, Emperor of Rome, 63 B.C.-14 A.D.
Claudius, Emperor of Rome, 10 B.C.-54 A.D.
Cleopatra, Queen of Egypt, d. 30 B.C.
Constantine I, Emperor of Rome, d. 337
Domitius Ahenobarbus, Cn., active 104-89 B.C. (Nero) and Octavia
Galba, Servius Sulpicius, Emperor of Rome, 3 B.C.-69 A.D.
Lysimachus, King of Thrace, ca. 361-281 B.C.
Maecenas, Gaius Cilnius, ca. 70 B.C.-8 B.C
Numa Pompilius, King of Rome, active 715-673 B.C.
Otho, Marcus Salvius, Emperor of Rome, 32-69
Philip II, King of Macedonia, 382-336 B.C.
Seneca, Lucius Annaeus, ca. 4 B.C.-65 A.D.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: These folders contain characters from classical mythologies of Greece and Rome: gods (e.g. Apollo), minor deities and divine beings (e.g., personifications, Sylvans), figures with little or no historical basis (e.g., Romulus), or are figures from literature (e.g., Dido from the Aeneid, scenes from Homer's Iliad, characters from Ovid's Metamorphoses). Characters have their own folders if prints were numerous, or characters were particularly well-known and therefore researchers were likely to look for them. The "miscellaneous" folders contain figures which did not fit easily into any category, including scenes from Greek and Roman life, and depictions of objects that were not busts. Busts that were not obviously identified as any particular figure are grouped together. Use the Oxford Classical Dictionary Online to determine whether characters are mythological or historical.

did.unittitle: Romulus, King of Rome, and Remus

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 28 boxes are British Isles. largest portion of the series.

did.unittitle: Plates from London Interiors (London: Joseph Mead, 1841-1844)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains plates removed from the two-volume publication; no text pages are present.

did.physdesc: Most of the houses in this set of folders are filed under the name of the house, but readers should also look under the owner's name. In only a few cases is the house filed under a location, such as Twickenham.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: See also Book Illustration - Life of Burns

did.unittitle: Palace of the Legion of Honor
Australia, New Zealand, and Île Amsterdam

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains prints formerly filed under Java, Malay Islands, Papua, and Timor.

did.unittitle: Spain, including Gibraltar and the Balearic Islands
Plates from Rae's Philadelphia Pictorial Directory and Panoramic Advertiser (Philadelphia: Julio H. Rae, 1851)
Ainsworth, William Harrison - Old St. Paul's
Browne, Hablot Knight - The Chronicles of Crime, edited by Camden Pelham Esq.
Byron, George Gordon Byron, Baron - Life of Byron

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes plates from Le Bravo,

did.unittitle: Cooper, James Fenimore - Last of the Mohicans
Defoe, Daniel - The Life and Adventures of Robinson Crusoe
Wilson, John - The Land of Burns

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A small group of prints, photographs, and printed material related to the American Civil War, Abraham Lincoln, and various military officers and associates of Lincoln. There are no original photographs of Lincoln in the collection.

did.physloc: Also stored in: Oversize Box XX

did.unittitle: Civil War officers and Lincoln associates
did.physloc: Also stored in: Oversize Box XX
Also stored in: Oversize Box XX

did.unittitle: Mathew B. Brady photograph: Headquarters, Army of the Cumberland, Chattanooga, Tennessee

did.physloc: Also stored in: Oversize Box XX
Also stored in: Oversize Box XX

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The folders contain prints, largely portraits, filed by the name of the engraver or lithographer, arranged as received with the collection in 1999. It is not known if the prints were grouped before or after arriving at Yale.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: One folder of small portraits of men engraved specifically for bank notes, stock certificates, and other financial or legal documents. Most are unattributed as to engraver or subject; some are credited to the American Bank Note Company, the Continental Basnk Note Company, and the New York Bank Note Company, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Holds single examples by engravers such as Gideon Fairman, Benjamin Tanner, and others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The series holds material related to the business and shop of Robert Fridenberg, including one folder of correspondence, folders of want lists, notes, and other lists, and examples of folders in which the shop housed prints. The single folder of letters contains inquiries from dealers, patrons, and engravers who did business with James Osborne Wright and Robert Fridenberg between 1893 and 1945, and include request lists from people seeking to extra-illustrate specific volumes.

There is no evidence of the condition and housing of the prints when the collection arrived at Yale University Library with the exception of the oversize prints and some of the topographical prints. The former arrived enclosed in "folders" made from text sheets of folio volumes that had been broken and the plates removed and trimmed, specifically the nine-volume set Galeries historiques du palais de Versailles (Paris: Imprimerie Royale, 1839-1853). Most of the topographical prints remained inside paper folders (many cut down from the Versailles volumes) which were then placed inside the covers of books which had also been broken and the plates removed for sale; Fridenberg retitled the spines with paper labels describing the prints held within, and these "folders," which could stand up on a shelf as a would a book, somewhat protected the merchandise from the layer of black soot that likely eminated from the shop's heating system. Among those broken sets were Aristide Guilbert's Histoire des Villes de France (Paris: Furne et Cie., 1845-48) and Evert A. Duyckinck's Portrait Gallery of Eminent Men and Women of Europe and America (New York: Johnson, Wilson, 1873). Examples of these and other hollowed-out book covers were kept with the collection for historical purposes.

did.unittitle: Want lists, lists, notes, and indexes

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The boxes hold several examples of the housing Fridenberg employed for storing his topographical prints on bookshelves in his shop.
